real.title: Partial dead-letter write leaves an orphan active file that poisons all future dead-lettering (within-run create_new collision)
real.claim: write_records creates dl_NNN.wab via WabSegment::create (create_new(true), segment.rs:59), writes/seals, and only bumps next_counter on full success (dead_letter.rs:80). A mid-write/seal failure leaves the partial .wab and does not advance the counter; commit_batch treats the dead-letter failure as transient (mod.rs:657/716) and retries, so write_records reuses the same counter and create_new fails with AlreadyExists, permanently breaking dead-lettering for the rest of the run.
real.verdict_reason: create_new(true) at segment.rs:59 + next_counter advanced only at dead_letter.rs:80 forces a same-counter AlreadyExists on retry; cross-restart half of the claim is wrong (recovery.rs:30-45 visits dead_letter/ and re-seals the partial), but the within-run poison is genuine.

real.title: Drain confirms+deletes a segment without verifying CommitResult covers every input record (committed + dead_lettered may not partition the batch)
real.claim: The Ok(commit_result) arm increments committed.len(), dead-letters dead_lettered, and returns BatchResult::Ok (→ Confirmed → confirm_and_delete deletes the segment) with no check that committed.len()+dead_lettered.len()==payloads.len(). The SDK contract (lib.rs:104-141) never requires the two vectors to partition the input, so a non-conforming external sink that drops a record from both vectors gets it silently confirmed-and-deleted: a false ack with zero detection.
real.verdict_reason: no accounting guard exists between mod.rs:612 and the Ok return at 662; built-in sinks (http.rs:335-364, SQL all-or-nothing) account for every record so only a contract-violating external sink triggers it, but the unguarded false-ack path is real.

real.title: Single permanently-rejected batch larger than dead_letter_max_bytes wedges the drain in a permanent block↔retry livelock
real.claim: The unblock condition (mod.rs:381) checks only total_bytes < cap, not that THIS batch fits. For a single oversized batch with an empty dir: unblock (0<cap) → retry → would_exceed_cap(0+estimated>cap) true (dead_letter.rs:57-58) → Blocked → repeat forever. No other pending segment drains while parked, and sink.commit() is re-called each interval re-POSTing the committed prefix.
real.verdict_reason: unblock predicate (mod.rs:381) and cap predicate (dead_letter.rs:57) use different conditions that can never both clear for an oversized single batch; reachable since config validation (config/mod.rs:589-601) only rejects a 0 cap and warns under 1 MiB.

real.title: Sink::health() is called with no timeout backstop; a hanging health() stalls the entire drain
real.claim: rt.block_on(sink.health()) at mod.rs:280/296/379 has no timeout, unlike commit (timeout at 593). A hanging health() wedges the single-threaded runtime: the idle loop (280) never returns to recv so shutdown is missed and segments pile up; at 379 it runs before the channel_closed check at 386 so shutdown-while-blocked is also missed.
real.verdict_reason: none of the three block_on(sink.health()) sites is wrapped in tokio::time::timeout, and 379 precedes the channel_closed branch at 386, so the commit hang-defense rationale is absent for health.

real.title: Retried multi-batch segment re-dead-letters earlier sub-batches, amplifying duplicate dead-letter files and thrashing the cap
real.claim: process_segment commits sub-batches sequentially (527-547). If an early sub-batch dead-letters successfully and a later one returns Transient/Blocked, the whole segment is preserved and retried from record 0 (re-opened at 485); the early sub-batch dead-letters again via write_records (no dedup, dead_letter.rs:64-83), duplicating records/files and, when Blocked, feeding a cap-overshoot loop.
real.verdict_reason: retry re-opens the segment from record 0 and write_records is unconditional, so any earlier dead-lettered sub-batch is rewritten each pass; duplicate dead-letter FILES + cap thrash are an untested amplification beyond the contracted duplicate sink delivery.

real.title: Dead-letter total_bytes silently undercounts on metadata() failure, bypassing the dead_letter_max_bytes cap
real.claim: After sealing, size is metadata(&sealed).map(len).unwrap_or(0) (dead_letter.rs:78). A failed stat yields 0 and total_bytes is not incremented despite a real file existing. total_bytes is the sole gate in would_exceed_cap (57-58) consulted at mod.rs:632/692, so the undercount lets writes exceed the cap (unbounded growth, never-tripped safeguard, under-reported gauge); rescan would fix it but only runs from the blocked wake (mod.rs:369) which the undercount prevents entering. Error is swallowed.
real.verdict_reason: unwrap_or(0) at dead_letter.rs:78 silently drops the stat error and skips the total_bytes increment, and would_exceed_cap has no other size source.

real.title: Dead-letter cap accounting omits fixed 60-byte per-file segment overhead, so dead_letter_max_bytes can be exceeded
real.claim: would_exceed_cap is checked against estimated_write_bytes=sum(len+8) (mod.rs:729-731), but write_records seals a full WabSegment of size estimated + 24(header) + 4(sentinel) + 32(footer) = estimated + 60 (format.rs:69-75). total_bytes is corrected with the real size (dead_letter.rs:78), so each write can creep ~60 bytes past the cap; over many tiny segments the slop is unbounded relative to the documented hard limit.
real.verdict_reason: format.rs constants confirm 24/4/32 overhead the estimate at mod.rs:729 omits, making the documented cap a soft bound; self-correcting per-write but never exactly enforced.

real.title: Swallowed rescan() error can wedge the drain blocked despite an operator-freed dead-letter directory
real.claim: While blocked, `let _ = dead_letter.rescan();` (mod.rs:369) discards the error. If read_dir fails inside scan_dir, total_bytes keeps its stale at-cap value, the unblock check at 381 stays false, and the drain stays blocked even after the operator cleared the dir, until a later rescan succeeds. No metric, no log.
real.verdict_reason: let _ = at mod.rs:369 drops the io::Result from rescan/scan_dir, and the unblock path at 381 depends entirely on the refreshed total_bytes.

real.title: dead_letter_full counter and blocked_since reset on every unblock→reblock cycle, inflating the metric and resetting blocked-duration
real.claim: An unblocked segment returns to Draining (384); if the next commit again returns Blocked it flows through transition_from_draining → next_state_after_process → enter_blocked (466), which increments dead_letter_full (468) and creates a fresh blocked_since (467). A flapping cap inflates weir_dead_letter_full past distinct episodes and repeatedly resets dead_letter_blocked_duration; the per-wake test does not cover the unblock/reblock cycle.
real.verdict_reason: re-entry to blocked via the Draining path goes through enter_blocked (mod.rs:468/467), re-incrementing the counter and resetting the duration each flap; only the within-stint per-wake case is tested (mod.rs:1708).

real.title: .confirmed sidecar created without explicit 0o600 mode, tripping the daemon's own recovery mode audit under any non-0o077 umask
real.claim: write_confirmed_durably uses std::fs::File::create (no mode, confirmed.rs:70), unlike segments (.mode(0o600), segment.rs:61). The .confirmed file (.wab.confirmed, format.rs:90) is only 0o600 because main.rs:181-183 sets umask(0o077). audit_segment_modes (recovery.rs:69-84) audits EXT_CONFIRMED files and flags mode != 0o600 as possible tampering, bumping weir_wab_unexpected_mode. Under any other umask the sidecar becomes group/world-readable and the daemon's own audit raises a false-positive alert on restart.
real.verdict_reason: confirmed.rs:70 has no .mode(), the file matches EXT_CONFIRMED audited at recovery.rs:71, and the 0o600 result depends solely on the umask set in main.rs:182.

real.title: DeadLetterWriter has zero unit tests; scan_dir counter-recovery across restart is untested
real.claim: dead_letter.rs has no #[cfg(test)] module; drain tests only open DeadLetterWriter and fault it by removing the dir. Untested behaviours gate data safety: scan_dir counter recovery across restart (open()/scan_dir parse counters and set next_counter=max+1), would_exceed_cap boundary/saturation, and rescan after external deletion. The system test only checks dead-letter metric names exist.
real.verdict_reason: grep confirms zero #[test]/mod tests in dead_letter.rs and confirmed.rs, and system.rs:358-362 only asserts metric NAMES; this is a genuine coverage gap leaving #3/#7 boundaries uncaught (severity high overstated for a pure test gap but the gap itself is real).

real.title: Segment counter resets to 1 after crash recovery; the new run's seal()-rename silently overwrites a still-undrained recovered .wab.sealed segment (data loss, crown-invariant violation)
real.claim: After Phase-1 recovery seals all .wab to .wab.sealed (recovery.rs:322), the flusher's scan_and_advance_counter (segment.rs:414) calls segment_counters (segment.rs:335) -> segment_counter_from_path which uses file_stem(); rustc-verified that seg_00000001.wab.sealed/.confirmed return None while seg_00000001.wab returns Some(1), so sealed/confirmed files are invisible and next_counter resets to 1. create_new/O_EXCL (segment.rs:59) only collides with an active .wab, not a .wab.sealed. The first new record creates seg_00000001.wab and on rotation/shutdown seal()'s std::fs::rename (segment.rs:258) atomically overwrites the recovered seg_00000001.wab.sealed (runtime-verified Unix rename overwrites). replay_unconfirmed only queues recovered segments (mod.rs:328-347); confirm_and_delete removes a sealed file only after a successful drain (confirmed.rs:42), so a slow/down sink leaves the recovered durably-acked-but-undelivered segment on disk to be destroyed — a retroactive false ack.
real.verdict_reason: rustc-confirmed file_stem() hides sealed/confirmed from segment_counters (segment.rs:335) so next_counter resets to 1, and runtime-confirmed std::fs::rename in seal() (segment.rs:258) overwrites the undrained recovered .wab.sealed.

real.title: segment_counters doc comment justifies skipping sealed files as 'matches the historical scan' — stale rationale that masks the counter-reset data-loss bug
real.claim: The SegmentStore::segment_counters doc (segment.rs:319-323) frames skipping sealed files as 'this matches the historical scan, which only advanced past active segments', making the sealed-skip look intentional and safe. Because recovery seals all active segments before any flusher scans, the scan can only ever observe an empty active set after a crash, so the rationale guarantees a counter reset rather than 'one past the highest existing segment' — the doc-facing facet of the critical counter-reset bug.
real.verdict_reason: the comment at segment.rs:319-323 presents the sealed-skip as safe-by-design, which directly masks the confirmed bug in finding 1.

real.title: recover_segment renames .wab -> .wab.sealed without fsync_parent_dir, unlike WabSegment::seal — recovered seal not crash-durable
real.claim: recover_segment writes sentinel+footer, calls file.sync_all() (recovery.rs:318), then fs::rename(path, &sealed) (recovery.rs:322) and returns at :329 with no fsync_parent_dir. The production seal path calls fsync_parent_dir(&sealed_path) after its rename (segment.rs:263), and the helper doc (segment.rs:518-528) states POSIX needs a parent-dir fsync to make a rename's dirent crash-durable. A crash during/just-after recovery can lose the .wab.sealed dirent; idempotent re-recovery+dedup means not data loss, but it violates the module's documented durability discipline. Fix: one fsync_parent_dir(&sealed)? after the rename. (Raw finding cited line 424; actual rename is recovery.rs:322.)
real.verdict_reason: recovery.rs:318-329 fsyncs the file but never the parent dir after the rename, whereas segment.rs:263 explicitly does, by the same module's documented rule.

real.title: replay_unconfirmed silently skips sealed-but-unconfirmed segments on a per-entry DirEntry error (filter_map(|e| e.ok()))
real.claim: replay_unconfirmed enumerates sealed segments with fs::read_dir(&sdir)?.filter_map(|e| e.ok()) (mod.rs:321-325), silently dropping any per-entry DirEntry Err, so that .wab.sealed is omitted from startup replay. Those segments hold records already durably acked under the crown invariant but never delivered. The crash-recovery scans were deliberately hardened to propagate dirent errors via .collect::<io::Result<Vec<_>>>()? (recovery.rs:30-32 and :99-101 with explicit comments), while this delivery-critical path still swallows them, with no log/metric. Recoverable on a later clean restart but delays at-least-once delivery indefinitely.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:321 uses filter_map(|e| e.ok()) (drops dirent errors) while recovery.rs:30-32/99-101 in the same subsystem deliberately propagate them — confirmed inconsistency on the durable-but-unconfirmed delivery path.

real.title: recover_open_segments processes the dead_letter/ directory as if it were a shard directory
real.claim: recover_open_segments iterates every subdir of wab_dir and only skips one named exactly 'quarantine' (recovery.rs:40). The dead-letter dir is <wab_dir>/dead_letter/ (dead_letter.rs:34) and holds segment-format files dl_NNNNNNNN.wab sealed to .wab.sealed (dead_letter.rs:69,75). A crash during write_records after create but before seal leaves an active dl_*.wab; on startup recovery treats dead_letter/ as a shard dir (is_dir, name != quarantine) and recover_shard_dir's filter (recovery.rs:103-106) matches the .wab-suffixed file and re-seals it, bypassing dead-letter accounting and dl_ counter ownership. Mostly benign because the format matches (re-sealed, not quarantined — quarantine only fires on bad magic/version/short-header), but unintended.
real.verdict_reason: recovery.rs:36-44 only denylists 'quarantine', so <wab_dir>/dead_letter/ (dead_letter.rs:34) is descended into and its torn dl_*.wab re-sealed by recover_shard_dir's .wab filter.

real.title: Recovery: no test for the oversized-payload_len boundary (record at exactly MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP must survive; one over must truncate)
real.claim: recover_segment treats a record whose length field exceeds MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP as corruption and truncates at the last valid record (recovery.rs:234) — a data-bearing recovery decision. Reviewing every recovery test (clean, empty, truncate-mid-record, bad-magic, unknown-version, multi-segment, check_confirmed variants, audit_segment_modes), none exercises the oversized-payload_len branch: neither the boundary (legit record at exactly MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP must be recovered) nor the just-over case (must truncate). The MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP tests in tests/system.rs:526-614 exercise the ingest/socket cap, not the recovery decode path. A future > vs >= off-by-one would silently drop the largest legal records on recovery with a green suite.
real.verdict_reason: the recovery.rs:234 > MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP branch has no test; the only MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP tests (system.rs:540/567) hit the socket layer, not recover_segment.

real.title: Recovery: the partial-seal sentinel branch during crash recovery is untested
real.claim: recover_segment has an explicit branch (recovery.rs:229) for a zero-length sentinel mid-recovery, documented (recovery.rs:226-228) as graceful handling of partial seals (sentinel written, footer/rename not). No recovery test constructs a segment with a written sentinel then runs recover_segment to assert it stops at the sentinel and recovers exactly the pre-sentinel records. The DST RenameFails scenario (dst.rs:499-510) uses finalize_to_disk (sentinel AND footer written+fsynced), not the sentinel-only mid-state; a tree-wide grep for build_sentinel/[0u8;4]-then-recover found nothing. Low because the same payload_len==0 decode path is exercised indirectly by SegmentReader round-trips and empty-segment recovery.
real.verdict_reason: no test in recovery.rs or tests/ writes a sentinel-without-footer mid-file then runs recover_segment; the DST RenameFails path uses fully-finalized segments (dst.rs:505).

real.title: macOS data fsync uses F_BARRIERFSYNC (barrier) while directory durability uses plain fsync via sync_all — weaker than F_FULLFSYNC; an explicit, undertested tradeoff on the crown durability path
real.claim: On macOS, record/seal durability uses platform_fsync = fcntl(F_BARRIERFSYNC) (segment.rs:506), a write barrier that orders writes but does not force a volatile-cache flush to the medium like F_FULLFSYNC; the parent-dir path uses File::open(dir)?.sync_all() i.e. plain fsync (segment.rs:535). Documented as 'sufficient for WAB durability' (segment.rs:503-505). The crown guarantee on macOS rests on barrier semantics, differs from the Linux fdatasync path, and is not power-loss validated (DST injects logical faults only). Flag for a precise per-platform 1.0 durability claim; F_FULLFSYNC needed for strict power-loss durability.
real.verdict_reason: segment.rs:500-511 and :532-538 match the finding exactly — F_BARRIERFSYNC for data, plain fsync for dir; an accurate, deliberate per-platform tradeoff correctly flagged as info, not a defect.

real.title: Metrics HTTP server has no read/write timeout — slowloris permanently wedges the unauthenticated endpoint and blinds monitoring
real.claim: handle() does a single untimed stream.read (server.rs:67) and untimed write_all (server.rs:87-88), bounded only by the max_connections semaphore (default 8, config/mod.rs:464) whose permit is held for the whole connection lifetime (server.rs:57-60). A silent peer parks the handler forever holding its permit; metrics_max_connections such connections exhaust the pool, after which every scrape hits try_acquire_owned -> Err (server.rs:52) and is dropped — observability permanently dead until restart. The data path guards this exact case with read_timeout (connection.rs:109/195/213) and a dedicated test; the metrics endpoint never got it.
real.verdict_reason: serve()/handle() (server.rs:43-89) contain zero tokio::time::timeout; the test endpoint_caps_concurrent_connections (server.rs:155-189) bakes in a silent conn1 holding the permit 'indefinitely' as expected behavior, while the data path guards the identical idle case at connection.rs:109.

real.title: Phase-2 coalesce starves co-located shards on a shared worker (worker_count < shard_count)
real.claim: When worker_count < shard_count (supported: shard_count 1..=256 config/mod.rs:395, worker_count 1..=64 config/mod.rs:398, sub-worker case documented 'still correct' config/mod.rs:243-249), one worker owns multiple shard buffers. Phase 2 (worker.rs:122-131) re-arms window per received record and flush_all() runs only after the loop exits (worker.rs:135). A flood on co-located shard X keeps the loop alive (sub-2ms gaps, COALESCE_MAX_US worker.rs:27), so a single record buffered for quiet shard Y waits unflushed for X's whole burst — cross-shard head-of-line latency coupling unbounded by batch_deadline.
real.verdict_reason: phase 2 loop (worker.rs:122-131) re-arms window per record and flush_all() is gated behind loop exit (worker.rs:135), so a sustained burst on one co-located shard delays a sibling shard's buffered record for the burst's full length, not bounded by batch_deadline.

real.title: accept_latency histogram uses 1ms-floor buckets for a sub-millisecond measurement
real.claim: weir_accept_latency_seconds is registered with LATENCY_BUCKETS (mod.rs:314-315), smallest bucket 1ms (mod.rs:273), but observed at socket/mod.rs:203 as accept_start.elapsed() (accept_start at socket/mod.rs:144) spanning only accept->semaphore->clones->spawn — microsecond-scale CPU work that always lands in the first bucket. The finer STAGE_BUCKETS that solves this exact problem (mod.rs:277-281) is bench-trace-gated (mod.rs:277), so accept_latency needs an always-compiled microsecond bucket set.
real.verdict_reason: accept_latency is wired to LATENCY_BUCKETS (1ms floor, mod.rs:273) while measuring microsecond-scale accept-to-spawn CPU work (socket/mod.rs:144 to 203); the finer STAGE_BUCKETS is #[cfg(feature=bench-trace)]-gated so the fix needs a new always-on constant.

real.title: flush_shard 'WAB is shutting down' comment misdescribes the permanent-shard-offline case
real.claim: flush_shard ends .ok(); // receiver gone means WAB is shutting down (worker.rs:168), but per the G-QUEUE-1 test (worker.rs:536-576) the receiver also disappears when a flusher panic-caps-out and its shard is permanently offline while the daemon keeps running. Behavior is correct (batches dropped, ack_tx dropped, producer sees Closed -> Nack, never a false ack), but the comment frames a recurring steady-state condition as a one-time teardown event.
real.verdict_reason: comment at worker.rs:168 says the dropped receiver means 'WAB is shutting down', but the G-QUEUE-1 test (worker.rs:536-576) proves the same path runs at steady state against a permanently-offline shard; behavior correct, comment inaccurate.

real.title: QueueSender exposes len() with no is_empty()
real.claim: QueueSender::len() is public (queue.rs:55-57, used by weir_queue_depth poll per queue.rs:53-54) with no companion is_empty() — the classic clippy::len_without_is_empty wart, latent because the workspace has no clippy lint config (no [lints] table in any Cargo.toml, no #![deny(clippy::...)] in lib.rs). len() also sums across partitions (queue.rs:56), so it is a cross-partition in-flight total, not a slot count — mildly misleading. Worth a deliberate decision before the 1.0 API freeze.
real.verdict_reason: len() exists at queue.rs:55-57 with no is_empty() (grep confirms none) and no clippy gate anywhere in the workspace, so the lint is latent; it is an info-level API-design decision for the 1.0 freeze, not a runtime defect.

real.title: UnknownMessageType/UnknownDurability decode errors are nacked as the (documented-as-transient, keep-connection-open) InternalError reason, yet the connection is closed
real.claim: UnknownMessageType/UnknownDurability (envelope.rs:188/190) arise on a CRC-valid header (the CRC check at envelope.rs:178-185 runs first), so they are permanent client protocol errors. nack_for_decode_error's `_ =>` arm (connection.rs:414) maps them to WireNack::InternalError + MetricNack::internal_error; the decode site then closes the connection (connection.rs:150). docs/wire_protocol.md:147 documents Nack(InternalError) as 'open — transient', so the wire contract is contradicted, the case is indistinguishable from a real pipeline InternalError (retry-loops forever), and the internal_error metric is polluted by malformed client input.
real.verdict_reason: envelope.rs:188/190 raise these post-CRC; connection.rs:414 routes them to InternalError and connection.rs:150 closes the connection, contradicting docs/wire_protocol.md:147 which marks InternalError 'open'.

real.title: Payload and CRC reads are not raced against handler_shutdown, so a mid-frame stall holds a semaphore permit until read_timeout during graceful shutdown
real.claim: The header read uses a tokio::select! with a shutdown_rx.changed() arm (connection.rs:107-114), but the payload read (195) and CRC read (213) are bare timeout(read_timeout, read_exact).await with no shutdown race. A client stalled mid-frame at shutdown is not woken by handler_shutdown_tx.send(true) (mod.rs:226) and sits until read_timeout. With defaults shutdown_timeout=read_timeout=ACK_TIMEOUT=30s, such handlers can consume the entire drain window and force the abort_all fallback (mod.rs:235-249), contradicting the stated 'exit immediately on next loop check' intent (mod.rs:229). The TCP handler shares the body (tcp.rs:222) and inherits the gap. No data loss.
real.verdict_reason: connection.rs:195/213 wrap read_exact in a bare timeout with no shutdown_rx.changed() arm, unlike the header read at connection.rs:107-114; defaults (config/mod.rs:469/482) make all three timeouts 30s.

real.title: Payload/CRC read timeout is a whole-transfer deadline but is documented and metered as a per-byte idle timeout
real.claim: timeout(config.read_timeout, read_exact(...)) makes read_timeout a total deadline for the whole payload/CRC phase, but ConnectionConfig::read_timeout is documented (connection.rs:43-47) as time 'waiting for the next byte' and the weir_connection_idle_timeout metric is described (metrics/mod.rs:322-323) as connections idle 'without receiving the next byte'. A slow-but-steady client streaming up to 16 MiB is killed at 30s (needing ~560 KB/s) though it never went idle, and connection_idle_timeout is incremented for it (connection.rs:199/217) — a non-idle condition. Narrow impact; mislabeling/semantics issue.
real.verdict_reason: the timeout wraps the entire read_exact (whole-transfer) while connection.rs:43-47 and metrics/mod.rs:322-323 both describe per-byte idle semantics, and the idle counter is bumped on a non-idle slow transfer.

real.title: Shutdown-time socket removal is path-based (std::fs::remove_file), inconsistent with the hardened unlinkat-based startup cleanup
real.claim: run() ends with std::fs::remove_file(&config.socket_path) (mod.rs:253) — a by-name unlink following the final path component at shutdown — reverting to the exact TOCTOU pattern the startup bind sequence was hardened against (dirfd + unlinkat + AT_SYMLINK_NOFOLLOW + inode-equality, mod.rs:343-415,484-530; docs/security/socket-bind.md:85,109-111). Under a group/world-writable parent (warned about at mod.rs:550-584), an attacker swapping an inode at the path before shutdown gets it unlinked. The listener fd is still held, so removal via the retained dirfd or skipping removal avoids acting on an attacker-controlled name. Bounded impact, low severity.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:253 uses path-based remove_file at shutdown, the precise pattern the startup unlinkat hardening (docs/security/socket-bind.md) was built to avoid, exploitable only under the already-flagged writable-parent case.

real.title: TLS handshake is not raced against handler_shutdown, so in-flight handshakes block graceful drain up to handshake_timeout
real.claim: tcp::run runs time::timeout(handshake_timeout, acceptor.accept(stream)) (tcp.rs:179-212) before passing handler_shutdown to handle_connection (tcp.rs:222). At shutdown handler_shutdown_tx.send(true) fires (tcp.rs:255) but mid-handshake tasks do not observe it, holding a permit and JoinSet slot until handshake completion or handshake_timeout (default 10s, config/mod.rs:618). A flood of TCP-connected-then-TLS-stalled clients at shutdown can consume the drain (shutdown_timeout 30s) and force abort_all (tcp.rs:267-277), inflating connections_aborted_at_shutdown. The Unix loop lacks this gap. Fix: select! the handshake against handler_shutdown.changed().
real.verdict_reason: the handshake at tcp.rs:179-212 runs before the shutdown-aware handle_connection (tcp.rs:222) with no shutdown race, so handshake_timeout (10s, config/mod.rs:618) gates clean drain.

real.title: umask save/restore in bind_hardened has no RAII guard; an unwind between set and restore would leak the tightened 0o177 umask process-wide
real.claim: bind_hardened does umask_set(0o177) (mod.rs:372), UnixListener::bind (373), umask_set(saved_umask) (374), then bind_result? (375). The restore is a plain statement, not a Drop guard; a panic/unwind between set and restore would leak umask 0o177 process-wide, silently tightening later file/dir creation. Latent today: only UnixListener::bind runs in the window and it returns Result (no panic path), but the invariant relies on that. An RAII guard (like OwnedDirFd, mod.rs:592-607) would make it unwind-safe; the existing test (mod.rs:785-802) covers only the success path.
real.verdict_reason: the restore at mod.rs:374 is a plain statement, not Drop-guarded; sound today only because UnixListener::bind (373) returns Err rather than panicking — a fragile, latent invariant for security-sensitive umask manipulation.

real.title: MySQL sink dead-letters live data on a recoverable connection-time auth/access failure (asymmetric with Postgres)
real.claim: MySqlSink::commit's pool.get_conn().await.map_err(classify) routes connection-acquisition failures through classify(), which for E::Server auth codes 1045/1044 returns permanent (is_transient_server_code false), so the whole batch is dead-lettered. Postgres forces every pool.get() failure to transient (postgres.rs:358-362), creating the asymmetry.
real.verdict_reason: mysql.rs:268 -> classify -> is_transient_server_code(1045/1044)=false (asserted mysql.rs:487-488) -> permanent -> dead-letter (mysql.rs:293-300), while postgres.rs:358-362 forces all pool errors transient.

real.title: ClickHouse response body read is not bounded by the sink's configured timeout (error path can hang to the drain backstop)
real.claim: timeout is applied only around req.send() (clickhouse.rs:280) and the reqwest client is built with no .timeout() (195-197), unlike the HTTP sink (http.rs:114). req.send() resolves on headers, so resp.text().await (308) reads the error body outside any sink-level timeout, hanging commit until the drain's coarse commit_timeout backstop fires.
real.verdict_reason: client built without .timeout() (clickhouse.rs:195-197) while http.rs:114 sets it; resp.text() at clickhouse.rs:308 is outside the tokio::time::timeout that wraps only req.send() (280).

real.title: ClickHouse split_credentials leaves username-only userinfo in base_url, contradicting the struct invariant
real.claim: split_credentials only strips userinfo when a ':' is present (clickhouse.rs:240); for http://user@host:8123 it falls through to (url, None) (248), leaving user@ in base_url, contradicting the documented base_url invariant (142-145) that userinfo is stripped and creds applied as basic auth.
real.verdict_reason: the rfind('@') block at clickhouse.rs:238 only strips inside the find(':') branch (240); a username-only authority falls through to (url, None) at 248, leaving user@ in base_url against the 142-145 invariant.

real.title: ClickHouse does not percent-decode URL credentials before HTTP basic auth (silent divergence from SQL sinks)
real.claim: split_credentials slices user/password substrings verbatim (clickhouse.rs:241-242) with no percent-decode, handed straight to basic_auth (277,327). A percent-encoded password (p%40ss) reaches ClickHouse literally, yields 401, status_is_transient(401) is false (220-224), and the batch is dead-lettered permanently — whereas SQL sink drivers percent-decode userinfo per RFC 3986.
real.verdict_reason: clickhouse.rs:241-242 slices raw substrings with no percent-decode then hands them to basic_auth (277); mysql_async::Opts::from_url (mysql.rs:149) and tokio_postgres Config parse decode per RFC 3986, creating the divergence.

real.title: ClickHouse content-derived dedup token silently breaks idempotency if sink_max_batch_size changes across a restart
real.claim: dedup_token() hashes the exact sub-batch handed to commit() (clickhouse.rs:72-84), and the drain re-splits each segment into sub-batches of sink.max_batch_size() (drain/mod.rs:505,527). A restart with a different sink_max_batch_size before replay regroups records into different boundaries, producing different tokens, so ClickHouse inserts duplicate rows. Surfaced nowhere operator-facing.
real.verdict_reason: token is derived from the mutable sub-batch grouping (clickhouse.rs:72-84) that the drain re-splits by max_batch_size() (drain/mod.rs:505,527); a changed batch size across restart yields different tokens and defeats dedup.

real.title: ClickHouse RowBinary encoding silently assumes a plain String column with no config validation and contract buried in a private fn comment
real.claim: encode_rowbinary() emits leb128(len)++bytes (clickhouse.rs:52-59), the RowBinary form for exactly one ClickHouse String column, stated only in the private fn comment (49-50). For FixedString/LowCardinality/Nullable the prefix is misparsed and the insert corrupted/rejected with no validation; the config (88-98) and module docs (1-19) never state the column must be plain String, unlike the SQL sink module docs.
real.verdict_reason: the String-column contract lives only in a private fn comment (clickhouse.rs:49-50); module docs (1-19) and config (88-98) omit it, unlike mysql.rs:10-16 / postgres.rs:17-26.

real.title: ClickHouse sink commit() request/response classification has no in-process test (only an #[ignore] live-docker integration test)
real.claim: Only the pure helpers are unit-tested; commit() itself (success->committed, transient status not confirmed, permanent->dead-letter, timeout mapping, dedup token on the wire) is exercised only by clickhouse_sink_end_to_end (system.rs:2096) which is #[ignore]+#[cfg(feature=clickhouse-sink)]+requires WEIR_TEST_CLICKHOUSE_URL, so a status-classification or dedup-token regression passes all running tests. The HTTP sink has a reusable in-process mock-server suite.
real.verdict_reason: commit() at clickhouse.rs:255 has zero in-process coverage; its only test is gated by #[ignore]+feature+env (system.rs:2093-2099), unlike http.rs's spawn_mock_server suite (http.rs:421).

real.title: HTTP concurrency: no test asserts a transient mid-batch error actually cancels still-in-flight POSTs
real.claim: The comment at http.rs:351-357 claims dropping the buffered stream cancels in-flight POSTs (load-bearing for the at-least-once double-delivery bound), but transient_in_middle_of_batch_returns_err (http.rs:699) uses 2 records and asserts only err.is_transient() (710), never counting requests that reached vs were dropped; the cancellation-on-drop behaviour is asserted nowhere.
real.verdict_reason: http.rs:699-711 asserts only that an Err is returned; no test counts server-side requests to prove later in-flight POSTs are actually cancelled.

real.title: HTTP concurrency: no test pins record-to-outcome pairing (right payload in committed vs dead_lettered) under concurrent POSTs
real.claim: mixed_batch_committed_and_dead_lettered (http.rs:684) sends 3 records at concurrency 8 but asserts only counts (695-696), never which payload landed where; the only content assertion (http_400_dead_letters, 616-618) is single-record. The comments at 685-686 are stale under concurrency 8 (all POSTs concurrent, response order non-deterministic); a regression mis-pairing record and outcome would not fail any test.
real.verdict_reason: http.rs:695-696 asserts counts only and the comment at 685-686 contradicts the concurrency=8 model; no content/identity assertion exists for the concurrent path.

real.title: weir-ctl `dl list` / `dl drop` silently see zero dead-letter segments (wrong suffix filter)
real.claim: dl_segments filters with `n.starts_with("dl_") && n.ends_with(".wab")` (main.rs:303), but DeadLetterWriter::write_records creates `dl_NNNNNNNN.wab` and immediately seals it to `dl_NNNNNNNN.wab.sealed` (dead_letter.rs:69-75; segment.rs:255-264). `.wab.sealed` does not end with `.wab`, so in steady state dl list always prints empty (main.rs:316-318) and dl drop --yes always prints nothing to drop (main.rs:338-340) — the whole dl subcommand no-ops. cmd_segments (main.rs:215-220) correctly tests `.wab.sealed`, so `segments` reports the same store as non-empty. No test for dl_segments.
real.verdict_reason: main.rs:303 requires `.ends_with(".wab")`; sealed dead-letter files end `.wab.sealed` (dead_letter.rs:69 + segment.rs:258/276), so the filter never matches in steady state.

real.title: CommitResult partition invariant (committed ∪ dead_lettered = batch) is unenforced; the drain confirms+deletes the segment unconditionally on Ok
real.claim: CommitResult<R> { committed, dead_lettered } (lib.rs:104-111) is a plain public-field struct with no constructor and no enforced invariant. The drain's Ok(commit_result) path uses committed.len() only for a metric (mod.rs:622), writes dead_lettered (mod.rs:624-660), then returns BatchResult::Ok (mod.rs:662) with NO reconciliation against payloads.len(); BatchResult::Ok confirms and deletes the segment. A third-party Sink that omits a record from both vectors causes a false confirm / silent loss, violating the crown invariant. First-party sinks (noop/postgres/http) all partition correctly, masking it today. The drain already preserves the segment defensively on a dead-letter write failure (mod.rs:648-658) but has no analogous guard for an under-covered CommitResult.
real.verdict_reason: No path computes committed.len()+dead_lettered.len()==payloads.len() before BatchResult::Ok (mod.rs:612-662); CommitResult (lib.rs:106-111) has no checked constructor. Severity reduced from critical: exploitation requires a misbehaving third-party sink, none in-tree.

real.title: SinkRecord::into_payload is documented as the dead-letter recovery path but is bypassed for whole-batch permanent (and transient) errors
real.claim: into_payload's doc (lib.rs:71) says it is used when a record must be dead-lettered, but the drain calls into_payload only for the per-record dead_lettered list of a successful CommitResult (mod.rs:625-629). On a whole-batch permanent error the drain writes the raw original payloads slice via dead_letter.write_records(payloads) (mod.rs:696), bypassing into_payload; the transient path never round-trips either. For a non-identity SinkRecord the dead-lettered contents on a permanent error are the original segment bytes, not into_payload output. The only impl in the workspace is the identity impl SinkRecord for Payload (lib.rs:77-85), so the divergence is latent today.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:696 dead-letters the raw payloads slice rather than into_payload output; the lib.rs:71 contract holds only for the identity Payload record.

real.title: Blocking client sets no socket read/write/connect timeouts; a wedged daemon blocks producers indefinitely
real.claim: connect/connect_with_default/from_stream (unix.rs:177-207) never set read or write timeouts and there is no connect timeout; connect_tls (tls.rs:37-74) likewise uses a plain blocking TcpStream::connect (tls.rs:70). read_response (unix.rs:136-160) blocks forever if the daemon accepts then never replies (flusher hang, SIGSTOP, half-open TCP). weir-ctl's scrape sets a 5s read timeout (main.rs:370), so the author knows the pattern; for a producer hot-path client the unbounded block is an availability hazard.
real.verdict_reason: No set_read_timeout/set_write_timeout/connect-timeout call exists in unix.rs:177-207 or tls.rs:70; read_response (unix.rs:138/146/150) uses blocking read_exact.

real.title: Client read_response allocates vec![0u8; payload_len] without the MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP guard the server applies before allocating
real.claim: read_response decodes the header then does vec![0u8; payload_len] (unix.rs:143-144) with payload_len up to u32::MAX before any read. The server's Envelope::decode rejects payload_len > MAX_PAYLOAD_HARD_CAP (16 MiB) before allocating (envelope.rs:270-276); the const is public in weir_core (lib.rs:34, version.rs:13) and the client already depends on weir-core. Header CRC covers only bytes [0..12] (envelope.rs:24), so a desync'd/coincidentally-valid header or a buggy daemon can force a multi-GiB allocation even though the next read_exact hits EOF. Real responses are tiny so it is not a practical attack today. The proptest never produces a valid header CRC, so the large-but-valid payload_len path is untested.
real.verdict_reason: unix.rs:144 allocates payload_len bytes with no cap, asymmetric with envelope.rs:271-276; only mitigated by tiny real daemon responses, hence medium.

real.title: weir-sink-sdk re-export doc describes Payload as bytes::Bytes, contradicting the R1 newtype rationale
real.claim: The doc on pub use weir_core::Payload (lib.rs:60-62) says 'a ref-counted bytes::Bytes (re-exported from weir-core)'. After R1, Payload is a newtype pub struct Payload(Bytes) (payload.rs:17), not an alias or a re-export of the bytes crate; payload.rs:7-15 states the reason (avoid leaking bytes semver into weir 1.0). A sink author trusting the comment would expect to pass a raw bytes::Bytes (won't compile without .into()) or to use Bytes inherent methods (only the Deref<[u8]> subset is available). Lone stale R1 survivor in soon-frozen public API doc.
real.verdict_reason: lib.rs:60-62 calls Payload a bytes::Bytes re-export; payload.rs:16-17 defines a newtype Payload(Bytes) deliberately to not leak the bytes crate.

real.title: SDK doc overstates that health() is called after every commit attempt
real.claim: Sink::health doc (lib.rs:149-151) says it is called after every commit attempt and on a wall-clock interval. In the drain, sink.health() runs after process_segment only in the Draining state (mod.rs:296-297). In RetryingTransient, process_segment runs (mod.rs:318-335) with no following health() call, so during a retry storm health is refreshed only by the periodic poll (mod.rs:277-282, mod.rs:379), not per attempt.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:303-336 RetryingTransient calls process_segment with no trailing sink.health(); only the Draining arm (mod.rs:296-297) probes health after a commit.

real.title: Client discards the daemon-version byte the server sends on a VersionMismatch Nack
real.claim: The server appends the daemon's WIRE_VERSION as the second Nack-payload byte on VersionMismatch (connection.rs:404-410 returns &[WIRE_VERSION]; 418-421 documents the [reason]++extra layout) so the client can render 'daemon is on wire protocol vN; this client is on vM'. The client's nack_error (unix.rs:210-218) reads only payload.first() and maps to ClientError::Nack(NackReason::VersionMismatch); Display (unix.rs:28) prints 'server nack: VersionMismatch', discarding the second byte. The cross-crate contract the server pays for is unfulfilled.
real.verdict_reason: connection.rs:409 sends &[WIRE_VERSION] as the second Nack byte; unix.rs:211 reads only payload.first() and never inspects payload[1].

real.title: Public error enums and CommitResult are not #[non_exhaustive]; adding any variant/field after 1.0 is a breaking change
real.claim: DecodeError (error.rs:13) and WeirError (error.rs:101) are exhaustive public enums with no #[non_exhaustive]; the same holds for ClientError (weir-client/unix.rs:11), CommitResult (sink-sdk:106, all-public fields) and SinkHealth (sink-sdk:115). The module doc at error.rs:8 says 'one variant per frame-validation step', so variant growth is the documented expectation — making every future validation step a major bump. Mark these #[non_exhaustive] before 1.0 (free now, impossible later). Correctly excludes the wire enums MessageType/Durability/NackReason whose repr is the contract.
real.verdict_reason: Workspace-wide grep for non_exhaustive returns zero hits; error.rs:13/101 are plain pub enums and CommitResult fields are all pub.

real.title: Envelope::new silently truncates payload length via payload.len() as u32, falsifying the documented 'cannot desync' invariant
real.claim: Envelope::new sets header.payload_len = payload.len() as u32 with no bound check (envelope.rs:221-225); encode() (240-247) still writes the full payload. For a >= 4 GiB payload the cast wraps and a decoder desyncs, contradicting the doc at 217-219 ('the two cannot desync'). The 16 MiB cap is enforced only on decode (envelope.rs:271) and at socket ingest (connection.rs:159-160), never on construction. Unreachable in the daemon and untested (proptests use 0..=256-byte payloads). Latent footgun + overstated doc claim frozen into the published 1.0 API.
real.verdict_reason: envelope.rs:223 casts payload.len() to u32 unchecked while encode() writes full bytes, so the 217-219 'cannot desync' doc is violable; severity lowered to low because a >= 4 GiB single payload is impractical and the daemon caps ingest at 16 MiB.

real.title: Header::new takes a payload_len argument that Envelope::new always overwrites — an API that invites desync
real.claim: Every production caller computes payload.len() as u32 and passes it to Header::new only for Envelope::new to overwrite it (unix.rs:90-92, connection.rs:431-437). For bare Header::encode without an Envelope (connection.rs:680-686), a caller can pass any value and produce a header whose declared length matches no payload — the desync the encapsulation work was meant to prevent. Dropping payload_len from Header::new (default 0, set by Envelope::new) would make the correct value the only reachable one.
real.verdict_reason: Callers at unix.rs:90-92 and connection.rs:431-437 pass a payload_len that envelope.rs:222-225 immediately discards; bare Header::encode at connection.rs:680-686 can fabricate a mismatched length.

real.title: Payload newtype: PartialEq impls and the Borrow<[u8]> hashmap-key contract are untested
real.claim: The three PartialEq impls (payload.rs:99/105/111) and the Borrow<[u8]> impl (payload.rs:61) have no direct test; the Borrow/Hash agreement (derive Hash at payload.rs:16 over inner Bytes, Borrow exposes &[u8]) holds today but is unpinned. A test asserting hash(Payload) == hash(&[u8]) plus the equality impls would pin the contract. NOTE: the finding's claim that the drain sha256 dedup depends on this Borrow consistency does not hold — no HashMap/HashSet is keyed by Payload and dedup uses content sha256 (sink/clickhouse.rs:72, sink/http.rs:47), not std Borrow/Hash.
real.verdict_reason: No test exercises payload.rs:61/99/105/111 (coverage gap is real), but the dedup rationale is refuted: grep finds no HashMap/HashSet keyed by Payload and dedup_token at sink/clickhouse.rs:72 uses Sha256 over bytes, not std Borrow.

real.title: Reserved flags byte is preserved verbatim on decode but never validated to be zero, foreclosing in-version flag evolution
real.claim: Header::decode reads flags = buf[7] (envelope.rs:191) and stores it without a == 0 check, despite the field doc (envelope.rs:91), lib doc, and docs/wire_protocol.md calling flags 'reserved; zero on write'. proptest_envelope.rs:170/180 asserts arbitrary nonzero flags round-trip, confirming a deliberate preserve-don't-reject choice. With WIRE_VERSION fixed at 1, a v1 daemon silently accepts and ignores any future flag bit, so flag semantics can only be added via a WIRE_VERSION bump. Worth an explicit decision/comment; rejecting nonzero flags now would preserve in-version flag evolution.
real.verdict_reason: envelope.rs:191 stores buf[7] with no validation and proptest_envelope.rs:170/180 proves nonzero flags decode cleanly, locking in the no-in-version-flag-evolution consequence.

real.title: shard_count in 65..=256 with defaulted worker_count rejects a documented, in-range config with a misleading error
real.claim: shard_count is validated to [1,256] (mod.rs:395) but worker_count to [1,64] (mod.rs:398), and worker_count defaults to shard_count (mod.rs:397). A shard_count in 65..=256 with no explicit worker_count makes worker_count inherit it, then check_range("worker_count", e.g. 128, 1, 64) fails naming a field the operator never set; the doc (mod.rs:245-249) recommends worker_count==shard_count, which is unreachable for shard_count in 65..=256.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:397 `unwrap_or(shard_count)` then mod.rs:398 `check_range("worker_count", worker_count, 1, 64)` rejects with the worker_count field name even when only shard_count was set.

real.title: Config-time warn! calls are silently dropped (tracing subscriber initialized after Config::load)
real.claim: Config::load() runs at main.rs:185; the only subscriber init is at main.rs:187-192, after. warn! emitted during config loading (file.rs:191/199 unknown-key net, mod.rs:596 dead_letter advisory) has no subscriber and is discarded, so TOML typos silently get defaults.
real.verdict_reason: main.rs:185 Config::load() precedes the sole tracing_subscriber::fmt()...init() at main.rs:187-192; warns at file.rs:191/199 and mod.rs:596 fire in that no-subscriber window.

real.title: Feature-gated [server] keys are silently dropped: in KNOWN_SERVER_KEYS but absent from RawConfig on builds without the feature
real.claim: KNOWN_SERVER_KEYS (file.rs:70-109) lists clickhouse/mysql/postgres keys unconditionally, but RawServer fields are cfg-gated (file.rs:37-54) with no deny_unknown_fields. clickhouse-sink is non-default, so sink_clickhouse_table is neither flagged by warn_unknown_keys (it IS known) nor read (field absent) — silently dropped. env.rs:40-45 is analogous for WEIR_SINK_CLICKHOUSE_TABLE.
real.verdict_reason: sink_clickhouse_table in KNOWN_SERVER_KEYS (file.rs:99) while RawServer field is #[cfg(feature="clickhouse-sink")] (file.rs:51-52), no deny_unknown_fields, and Cargo.toml default excludes clickhouse-sink.

real.title: HELP advertises feature-gated CLI flags that fail as generic 'unknown arguments' when the feature is off, with no feature hint
real.claim: HELP lists --sink-mysql/postgres/clickhouse flags unconditionally (cli.rs:42-52) but parsing is cfg-gated (cli.rs:146-181); with the feature off the flag is unconsumed and hits the generic 'unknown arguments' error (cli.rs:200-206). Only --sink-clickhouse-database (cli.rs:49-50) carries the feature caveat; --sink-clickhouse-table/-column (cli.rs:51-52) do not, unlike SinkType::parse which gives a precise feature error.
real.verdict_reason: cli.rs:51-52 list the flags with no feature caveat while cli.rs:170-181 gate their parsing under clickhouse-sink; unconsumed flags reach the generic error at cli.rs:200-206.

real.title: Boolean env/CLI values accept only exactly 'true'/'false'; '1', '0', 'TRUE' abort startup
real.claim: peer_uid_check and sink_send_idempotency_key are parsed via env_parse (env.rs:19,26) and opt_value_from_str (cli.rs:123-125,140-142), both using FromStr for bool, which accepts only literal true/false. WEIR_PEER_UID_CHECK=1 or --peer-uid-check 0 aborts startup; peer_uid_check is a security-relevant default-on toggle and help shows only <bool> (cli.rs:30). Fails safe but is a footgun.
real.verdict_reason: env.rs:79-80 `v.parse::<T>()` and cli.rs:123 opt_value_from_str both route through FromStr for bool, whose only accepted inputs are the literals true/false.

real.title: log_level is never validated; invalid or empty values silently degrade or disable logging
real.claim: log_level is passed through unvalidated (mod.rs:611). main.rs:188-191 falls back to info on parse error, so a typo silently degrades; worse, WEIR_LOG_LEVEL="" returns Some("") (env.rs:57-60), wins the merge, and EnvFilter::try_new("") succeeds with no directives — logging effectively OFF, with no diagnostic.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:611 applies no validation; empty-string env (env.rs:57-60 Some("")) wins merge and reaches EnvFilter::try_new at main.rs:188-191 with silent fallback/off behavior.

real.title: Config derives Debug, exposing sink_url credentials, inconsistent with the project's deliberate bearer_token redaction
real.claim: Config derives Debug (mod.rs:236) over sink_url (mod.rs:295), which holds DSNs with credentials (cli.rs:67-70). The sink layer wrote manual redacting Debug impls for exactly this concern (http.rs:78-92 bearer_token; sql_common.rs:90-91, mysql.rs:86, postgres.rs:102 redact sink_url password). Config gets a naive derive. Currently only field-level logging exists (main.rs:346,371), so this is latent, but a future debug!(?config) or panic would leak DB credentials.
real.verdict_reason: mod.rs:236 #[derive(Debug)] over sink_url (mod.rs:295) contradicts the deliberate DSN-password redaction at sql_common.rs:90-91/mysql.rs:86/postgres.rs:102; no whole-Config log exists today so risk is latent.

real.title: u64 durations range-checked via `as usize` truncate on 32-bit targets, letting out-of-range values pass
real.claim: batch_deadline_ms (mod.rs:406), connection_read_timeout_secs (mod.rs:485), sink_timeout_secs (mod.rs:530), dead_letter_check_interval_secs (mod.rs:606) are u64 but range-checked via `value as usize`. On 32-bit usize a u64 above u32::MAX wraps before the bound check (2^32+1 -> 1, passes), then is stored as the full u64 — validated range and runtime value disagree. Harmless on 64-bit servers weir targets, hence info.
real.verdict_reason: all four sites cast u64 as usize before check_range (mod.rs:406,485,530,606); truncation is reachable only on 32-bit usize, and weir targets 64-bit, so info severity is correct.

uncertain.title: MySQL transient code 1317 (ER_QUERY_INTERRUPTED) is undocumented in module + classify docs and untested
uncertain.claim: is_transient_server_code() matches 1317 but the module docstring (mysql.rs:30-36) and the classify narrative enumerate only 1205/1213/1290/1062, and the is_transient_server_code_table test (mysql.rs:476-490) never asserts 1317.
uncertain.verdict_reason: 1317 is genuinely absent from the module docstring (mysql.rs:27-39) and the test (mysql.rs:476-490), but it IS documented at docs/operations/configuration.md:718, so 'undocumented' is only partly true and impact is info-level not low.

refuted.title: Concurrent HTTP sink discards already-accumulated dead_lettered records when a later record in the same batch hits a transient error
refuted.claim: On the first transient outcome commit() does return Err(e) (http.rs:357), dropping the partially-built dead_lettered vec; on retry permanently-rejected records re-POST and can be dead-lettered repeatedly, and an always-400+always-503 mixed batch makes no forward progress — a behavioral regression the serial design lacked.
refuted.verdict_reason: http.rs:351-358 implements the documented 'transient aborts the segment so the drain retries' contract; at-least-once holds (nothing falsely confirmed) and a serial per-record loop returning Err on the first transient would behave identically, so it is intended behaviour, not a regression.
